By generating CoT-guided affordance pixel that re-grounds navigation onto the nearest traversable pathway at every step, ABot-N1 recovers safe, compliant trajectories that metric-only policy cannot maintain. ABot-N1 (92.0% SR / 90.7% SPL) already outperforms all baselines, confirming the framework advantage independent of multi-task data; the full ABot-N1 further improves all metrics, especially on Medium and High tiers, suggesting that social-rule semantics learned via object-goal and POI-goal supervision transfer back into point-goal compliant behavior. Indoor performance under the strict no-collision criterion shows ABot-N1 at 95.4% SRa +5.8-point gain over ABot-N0 (89.6%)with the improvement concentrated on the High-difficulty set (95.8 vs. 87.5), indicating robust generalization to complex interior layouts.

sections.title: 6.1.4 POI-Goal: ABotN-POIBench
sections.content: Protocol. We evaluate on ABotN-POIBench (Sec. 5.2). The success criterion is final-meters arrival to the annotated entrance frame: an episode is successful if the agent terminates within 2 radius of the entrance (SR<2m). We additionally report SPL against the reference trajectory. Analysis. ABot-N1 sets new state of the art on ABotN-POIBench, raising SR<2m from 42.3% (POINav) to 77.3% (+35.0 points) and SPL from 40.3% to 72.6% (+32.3 points). Even ABot-N1 variant already achieves 69.9% / 59.8%, confirming the framework advantage alone; the full ABot-N1 pushes further, benefiting from the 24 Table 5 Quantitative POI-Goal Results on the Urban-Scale ABotN-POIBench. We report the entrance arrival success rate (SR<2m) and SPL compared against representative baselines. Our proposed ABot-N1 establishes new SOTA, boosting the success rate to 77.3% and achieving 72.6% SPL. Method SR<2m SPL ViNT [47] OmniNav (vanilla) [65] OmniNav (BridgeNav training)[65] ABot-N0 [1] POINav [23] ABot-N1 ABot-N1 19.0 23.9 34.4 20.9 42.3 69.9 77.3 18.2 22.4 31.5 17.7 40.3 59.8 72. largest cross-task synergy we observe: POI-goal supervises only the slow system, so its fast controller benefits from point-goal data, while its slow system benefits from object-goal -style visibility reasoning. The contrast with OmniNav variants is instructiveOmniNav (BridgeNav training) improves over the vanilla version by 10 points but plateaus, consistent with the documented BridgeNav rollout-bias on large-heading-offset starts; ABot-N1s slow system, trained on 3.0 tri-view annotations including 0.5 hard negatives, avoids this trajectory bias by construction.

sections.title: 6.1.5 Person-Following: EVT-Bench
sections.content: Protocol. We evaluate person-following on the closed-loop EVT-Bench [58] in Habitat, using the identical protocol as ABot-N0 [1] and TrackVLA [58]: each episode requires the agent to follow target person under three difficulty levels Single-Target Tracking (STT, nominal following), Distracted Tracking (DT, similarlooking pedestrians causing identity confusion), and Ambiguity Tracking (AT, frequent severe occlusions). For each scenario we report Success Rate (SR), Tracking Rate (TR, formerly following rate), and Collision Rate (CR), all in percent. Analysis. ABot-N1 leads on all SR metrics and on TR for the DT and AT splits, while matching the top STT TR within 0.2 points. On the easy STT setting, it reaches 90.1 SR / 89.8 TRsurpassing ABot-N0 by +3.2 SR and +2.2 TR while maintaining comparable CR (4.27 vs. 8.54). On the hardest AT split, ABot-N1 achieves 70.0 SR / 87.8 TR, improving over ABot-N0 by +2.7 SR and +8.3 TR, confirming that the asymmetric pixel protocol (target-pixel set to null while the affordance pixel falls back to the first projectable future waypoint, cf. Sec. 4.2.2) effectively hardens re-identification across severe occlusions. The most striking gain appears on DT, where ABot-N1 raises TR from 75.4 to 84.4 (+9.0 points) and SR from 66.7 to 67.4, indicating that joint multi-task training strengthens the slow systems identity reasoning even under distractor interference. The trade-off is slightly higher CR on DT/AT (17.9% vs. 11.6% / 7.05%), reflecting more aggressive approach policy learned from the combined training set; future work may mitigate this via an explicit collision-avoidance reward during post-training. 25 Table 6 Quantitative Person-Following Results on EVT-Bench [58]. SR/TR in %, higher is better; CR in %, lower is better. The symbols and indicate the adoption of GroundingDINO and the SoM+GPT-4o pipeline, respectively, as in ABot-N0 [1]. ABot-N1 achieves the best SR and TR across the three splits with the sole exception of STT TR, surpassing ABot-N0 and all baselines. sections.title: 6.1.6 Cross-Task Summary
sections.content: Across the five tasks evaluated in this report, ABot-N1 improves over ABot-N0 on the primary metric of every task: +3.9 SR on R2R-CE, +16.0 SR on ABotN-PointBench (outdoor split) with simultaneously improved compliance, +0.558 absolute jump in SR<2m on ABotN-POIBench, and +3.2 STT-SR / +2.7 AT-SR on EVT-Bench Person-Following. On short-horizon OVON, ABot-N1 variant lifts SR by +12.3, confirming the framework advantage even without multi-task data. ABot-N1 matches or exceeds ABot-N1 on point-goal, instruction-following, and POI-goal and only marginally trails on object-goal, indicating that the unified pixel-goal interface supports positive cross-task transfer and effective scaling rather than the destructive interference often reported when training generalist navigators on heterogeneous data.

sections.title: 6.2 Real-World Deployment
sections.content: To validate the practical applicability of the proposed system, we deploy the complete ABot-N1 on our proprietary AMap TuTu quadrupedal robot platform and conduct extensive real-world experiments in diverse indoor and outdoor environments.

sections.title: 6.2.1 Hardware Setting
sections.content: The experimental platform is AMap TuTu, 12-DOF quadrupedal robot equipped with omnidirectional LiDAR, RTK-GNSS, and Tri-Camera array spanning 270 horizontal FOV for egocentric perception. For ABot-N1, we introduce two key upgrades. First, we deeply integrate the previously modular perception stack (BEV occupancy mapping, legged odometry, and obstacle filtering) with the VLN inference pipeline into single unified onboard framework, eliminating inter-process communication overhead and reducing end-to-end latency. Second, we upgrade the compute module from Jetson Orin NX to Jetson AGX Orin 64 GB. The 4 memory expansion and increased GPU throughput are critical for hosting the dual slow-fast VLN inference concurrently with real-time BEV perception and locomotion control, enabling the full navigation architecture to run entirely on-robot without offloading.

sections.title: 6.2.2 Edge Deployment Scheme
sections.content: For real-time execution on the edge device, we deploy the full slow-fast pipeline at an overall control frequency of 10 Hz. To ensure robust performance under limited computational resources, lightweight model structure together with an asynchronized sequential execution scheme are deliberately designed as follows. To reduce parameter size, the slow system is built upon Qwen-3.5-2B, while the fast system is implemented by DiT with 306 parameters whose visual observations are encoded by DINOv2-Base encoder. The two 26 Figure 10 Point-Goal Deployment. Four segments of long-range outdoor episode showcasing obstacle avoidance on narrow roads, construction area detour, correct fork selection, and traffic-light-compliant crosswalk traversal. systems operate asynchronously: the slow system runs at lower frequency than the fast system, whereas the fast system runs continuously with cached information from the slow system and current observations over multiple control cycles. Once the slow system produces an updated plan, the fast system generates waypoints conditioned on the latest reasoning result. This decoupled scheduling maximizes GPU utilization on the Jetson AGX Orin while maintaining consistent 10 Hz closed-loop responsiveness.

sections.title: 6.2.3 Deployment Results
sections.content: We present qualitative deployment results across the five core tasks, demonstrating ABot-N1s real-world reasoning and control capabilities through representative episodes with slow-system CoT and pixel-level prediction visualizations. Figure 10 illustrates long-distance outdoor navigation episode decomposed into Point-Goal Navigation. four representative segments. (1) Narrow-road obstacle avoidance: the agent successfully navigates around parked vehicles on constricted roadway, with the affordance pixel consistently anchored to the traversable lane. (2) Construction-zone detour : upon detecting construction area, the slow system places the affordance pixel on detour around the restricted region, steering the fast controller clear of the illegal construction zone rather than entering it. (3) Fork selection: at road bifurcation, the affordance pixel is anchored to the target-aligned branch, steering the agent onto the correct path and avoiding wrong-turn failures. (4) Traffic-light compliance: approaching signalized crosswalk, the agent waits for the green signal before crossing, demonstrating awareness of traffic rules and the ability to temporally gate its own actionsa behavior absent from metric-only point-goal policies. These results confirm that the slow systems traversability-aware pixel guidance translates into safe, rule-compliant outdoor navigation beyond simple goal-reaching. Figure 11 presents three representative object-goal episodes with CoT, affordance Object-Goal Navigation. pixel, and target pixel outputs. (1) Outdoor bench finding: the agent locates park bench under dappled tree shade at considerable distance, demonstrating robust long-range recognition under complex natural lighting. (2) Indoor chair with water bottle: the slow system reasons about spatial relations between objects distinguishing the target chair with water bottle from other chairsevidencing spatial reasoning and attribute-aware recognition. (3) Indoor fire extinguisher : the agent navigates around indoor obstacles and identifies partially occluded fire extinguisher, showcasing perception under occlusion. Across all cases, the 27 Figure 11 Object-Goal Deployment. Three casesoutdoor bench under dappled tree shade at long range, indoor chair with water bottle (spatial reasoning), and partially occluded fire extinguisherwith CoT, affordance, and target pixel overlays. Figure 12 POI-Goal Deployment. Locating Lanzhou noodle restaurant (large viewing angle), McDonalds (obstacle avoidance en route), and Luckin Coffee (slope navigation and staircase avoidance). affordance pixel consistently falls on safe traversable surfaces while the target pixel locks onto the goal object once visible, validating the decoupled recognition-and-approach protocol. Figure 12 shows three POI-goal episodes in which the agent locates commercial POI-Goal Navigation. storefronts by recognizing signage. (1) Lanzhou beef noodle restaurant: the agent identifies the shop sign at large oblique viewing angle, demonstrating robust text and logo recognition under perspective distortion. (2) McDonalds: building upon recognition, the agent performs obstacle avoidance during approach, coordinating the affordance pixel around street-level obstructions. (3) Luckin Coffee: the agent navigates gentle slope, identifies passable entrance, and avoids an impassable staircase with high steps, demonstrating the slow systems joint reasoning over accessibility and goal identity. These results highlight that POI-goal requires not only fine-grained visual recognition but also path-feasibility reasoning, both of which are naturally captured by the target pixel + affordance pixel protocol. Figure 13 visualizes four key decision moments during an indoor instruction-following Instruction-Following. episode with the command: Walk down the stairs . . . approach and stop directly in front of the bar counter. At each moment the slow system produces CoT trace and an affordance pixel (and, near the destination, 28 Figure 13 Instruction-Following Deployment. Slow-system reasoning at four critical momentsstair descent, gym entry, gym exit, and bar approachwith CoT, affordance pixel, and target pixel visualizations. target pixel): (1) Descending stairs: the CoT identifies the current sub-instruction and the affordance pixel marks the stairwell path. (2) Entering the gym room: the reasoning correctly advances the instruction pointer and anchors the pixel to the gym doorway. (3) Exiting the gym: the CoT tracks cumulative progress and shifts the pixel toward the hall exit. (4) Approaching the bar counter : having recognized the final landmark, the system emits target pixel directly on the bar and prepares to terminate. The explicit CoT decomposes the long instruction into manageable sub-goals at each step, eliminating the ambiguous mid-segment behavior typical of single-system VLN policies. Figure 14 illustrates three Person-Following cases. (1) Outdoor following with distractors: Person-Following. the agent maintains stable tracking of the designated target despite the presence of other pedestrians, demonstrating the slow systems robust identity reasoning and re-identification capability. (2) Outdoor stairclimbing: the target ascends staircase and the agent follows seamlessly, confirming the fast systems stable low-level control under elevation changes. (3) Indoor corner tracking: as the target rounds sharp indoor corner, the agent adjusts its heading in time and follows smoothly while keeping the target continuously in view. The combination of slow-system identity persistence and fast-system reactive control ensures continuous, collision-aware following across challenging real-world conditions. Summary. The five deployment tasks collectively validate the slow-fast architecture: the slow system provides high-level semantic reasoninginstruction decomposition, open-vocabulary recognition, spatial relation inference, rule-aware decision-making, and identity persistenceexternalized as CoT traces and pixel-level grounding; the fast system translates these pixel anchors into smooth control with precise last-meter arrival, dynamic obstacle avoidance, and stable locomotion under terrain variation. Their asynchronous coupling eliminates odometric drift through continuous visual re-grounding while the fast systems reactivity compensates for the slow systems latency, yielding robust, compliant navigation across all five real-world scenarios. 29 Figure 14 Person-Following Deployment. Outdoor tracking under pedestrian distraction, stair-climbing following, and indoor corner-rounding with temporary occlusion.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: We have presented ABot-N1, general visual-language navigation foundation model whose fastslow architecture factorizes high-level cognition from low-level control: slow vision-language reasoner produces Chain-of-Thought traces and pixel-level grounding, while fast action expert converts these into reactive waypoints. The dual visual-language guidance through affordance and target pixels serves as universal action interface that accommodates five navigation taskspoint-goal, instruction-following, object-goal, POI-goal, and person-followingfrom single multi-task checkpoint, enabling strong cross-task transfer without task-specific modifications. ABot-N1 achieves state-of-the-art results on all five benchmarks (70.89% SR on R2R-CE, 92.9% SR on outdoor point-goal, 77.3% on POI-goal, 84.9% on object-goal, and 90.1% on person-following), with the multi-task model consistently matching or exceeding single-task specialists. Real-world deployment on quadrupedal robot further confirms generalization from simulation to diverse physical environments, demonstrating viable path toward embodied navigation agents that are simultaneously robust, versatile, and interpretable.
